macro_segments.title: Tripping as negligence: The foundational case of two potters and the debate over negligence requirements

micro_segments.title: Opening: Two potters walking one behind the other—basic case of liability for tripping
Rabbi Yochanan's position: Tripping is not inherent negligence; liability requires standing or warning
Three frameworks for understanding passive person's liability: Bor vs. Adam HaMazik and agency
Debate over timing: Must the fallen person have opportunity to stand, or is warning sufficient?

macro_segments.title: The stopping beam case: Right to stop in the road and warning obligations as alternative liability grounds

micro_segments.title: Beam and barrel collision: When one party stops legitimately vs. improperly in the roadway
Distinguishing stopping to adjust load from unjustified break: Degrees of liability and warning requirements
Tosafot's challenge: If people must anticipate stops, why does reason for stopping matter?

macro_segments.title: Multi-car pile-up: Cascading liability in sequential tripping and role of opportunity to act

micro_segments.title: Baraita on potters and glass blowers: First liable for second, second for third in sequence
Warning as exemption: If one warns the next person, liability is avoided despite opportunity to stand
Gemara's defense of R. Yochanan: Liability only when opportunity to stand arises, warning alone insufficient

macro_segments.title: Rava's resolution: Distinguishing person's body from person's property in determining liability categories

micro_segments.title: First person liable for both body and property damage (Adam HaMazik); second person liable only for body damage (Bor)
Parsing the distinction: Is this about what damages whom, or about who is doing the damaging?
Rashi's explanation: Degree of negligence determines whether one is Adam HaMazik or Bor—agency through choice

macro_segments.title: The broken vessel problem: Reconciling Rava with Baraita using mafkir nezikav and the status of fallen objects

micro_segments.title: Gemara's challenge: Baraita exempts all from property damage; how does Rava's first person liability fit?
Debate over whether a person can be a Bor: Rav vs. Shemuel on ownership and Hefker status
Resolution: Distinction between vessel-on-vessel damage (Bor exempt) and body damage; mafkir nezikav after ones
Alternative reading: All persons are Adam HaMazik; they're exempt on vessels because vessels are Bor

macro_segments.title: All fell because of the first: Different scenarios for complete causation and spread across the roadway

micro_segments.title: If everyone tripped over the first person directly, first is liable for all damages
Rav Pappa's image: First person spread across width of road like a carcass (horizontal)
Rav Zevid's image: First person at an angle like blind person's staff; third person cannot see true hazard location

topical_tags.term: Nitkal Poshea (one who trips is negligent)
Adam HaMazik (active damager with inherent liability)
Bor (pit/passive hazard exempt in certain contexts)
Patur bekelim (Bor exempt for vessel damage)
Mafkir nezikav leachar ones/nefila (abandonment of liability after force majeure)
Rabbi Yochanan vs. other authorities on tripping liability
Rava's distinction: First person as Adam HaMazik, second as Bor
Rashi's explanation: Agency and degree of negligence determine status
Tosafot's challenge to stopping liability framework
Baraita on potters and glass blowers exemption
Rav Pappa: Horizontal spread (paska l'orkah k'shilda)
Rav vs. Shemuel on whether person can be Bor
Shemuel: All hazards are Bor (kol takala bor hu)
